title: Random Character Varieties
speaker: Emmanuel Breuillard
abstract: Irreducibility of random polynomials of large degree has been studied recently in works by several authors (in particular by Bary-Soroker, Kozma, Koukoulopoulos and by Varju and myself). We study analogous problems in the setting of word maps in matrix groups, such as SL2(C) or more general semi-simple Lie groups. Conditionally on GRH, we are able to determine the dimension and number of components of word varieties  with an exponentially small probability of exceptions. We also exhibit a phenomenon of Galois rigidity when the defect of the random presentation is equal to one.
tags: Workshop on Additive Combinatorics and Algebraic Connections

proof_stages.label: Representation and character-variety setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines Hom(Γ,G) for a finitely presented group Γ and semisimple algebraic group G, forms the GIT character variety by conjugation, and isolates the open locus X_Γ of Zariski-dense representations.

proof_stages.label: Benchmark questions and known counting cases
proof_stages.summary: The speaker poses the basic problems of dimension, components, Galois action, and special loci, then reviews rigid higher-rank lattices and surface groups, where finite-field counting and the Frobenius-Mednykh formula give irreducibility and dimension but rely on the special surface relator.

proof_stages.label: Random-relator model and main theorem
proof_stages.summary: Random groups are introduced by choosing r independent relators of length l, and the main conditional theorem states high-probability emptiness for r≥k, finite nonempty single-Galois-orbit behavior for r=k−1, and absolute irreducibility of expected dimension (k−r−1)dim G for r<k−1, under a GRH/no-Siegel-zero type hypothesis.

proof_stages.label: Uniform nonidentity consequence and qualitative implications
proof_stages.summary: The theorem is related to uniform exponential decay of the probability that a random word vanishes on any Zariski-dense generating tuple, with discussion of what such estimates imply and how they compare with many-relator random-group results.

proof_stages.label: Chebotarev-Lang-Weil point-count criterion
proof_stages.summary: For a variety over Q, averaged normalized counts of F_p-points over primes recover dimension and the number of Q-irreducible components, via Lang-Weil estimates, Frobenius fixed points on components, Burnside/Cauchy’s fixed-point lemma, and an effective Chebotarev input.

proof_stages.label: Averaging point counts over random presentations
proof_stages.summary: Since arbitrary relators have no Frobenius formula, the proof averages #Hom(Γ,G(F_p)) over random Γ, rewriting it as a sum over generating k-tuples of probabilities that each random relator evaluates to the identity, which should behave like |G(F_p)|^{k-r} before quotienting by conjugation.

proof_stages.label: Uniform expansion and mixing input
proof_stages.summary: The required uniform equidistribution is supplied by an expander theorem for all relevant Cayley graphs of G(F_p), outside a sparse exceptional set of primes, ensuring random walks mix fast uniformly in the generating tuple and making the averaged point-count estimates effective.

examples.name: Free two-generator SL2(C) character variety
examples.note: The speaker recalls that the SL2(C) character variety of F2 is C^3 with trace coordinates, making the definition of character variety and the Zariski-dense open locus concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: The free two-generator SL2(C) character variety is explicitly named and described as C^3 with trace coordinates, but the speaker does not derive or compute this description in the talk.

passes.reason: A standard Fricke–Klein free-group character variety is used as a basic model, not as a sporadic or adversarial case.
the free two-generator SL2(C) character variety is the no-relations base case, giving the unconstrained affine space C^3 rather than a constrained character variety
It is identified with affine 3-space via trace coordinates, a maximally regular character variety used as a basic example.
The free two-generator case has no relators and gives the unconstrained SL2 character variety C^3, a maximal and highly regular base case.
Fricke–Klein identifies it with the highly regular affine space C^3, the unconstrained/maximal two-generator SL2 character variety.
The free two-generator case has no relator constraints and gives the affine-space base character variety X(F2,SL2)≈A^3.
the no-relator rank-two free case is the maximally unconstrained base case, with SL2 character variety C^3
Used as a basic illustrative Fricke--Klein character variety, with no counterexample role and no sporadic or extremal structure.
The free two-generator case has no relator constraints and gives the universal affine C^3 character variety, a base-case/maximal-dimension example rather than a refutation.
The Fricke--Klein example is the relation-free/base case, with the SL2 character variety of F2 collapsing to affine 3-space rather than a constrained variety.
For F2 there are no relators, so the SL2 character variety is the maximally unconstrained affine space C^3.
the free rank-two case has no relators and gives the unconstrained/maximal-dimension SL2 character variety A^3

examples.name: Higher-rank lattice SL_n(Z)
examples.note: The case Γ=SL_n(Z), n≥3, illustrates rigidity by giving finite character varieties via superrigidity, as a contrast with positive-dimensional cases.
examples.engagement_reason: SL_n(Z) for n≥3 is cited as a higher-rank lattice example whose character variety is finite by rigidity, with no concrete calculation or verification.

passes.reason: In the character-variety context, superrigidity makes the associated Zariski-dense character variety finite, essentially a collapsed rigid case.
SL_n(Z) for n>=3 is a standard higher-rank arithmetic lattice used to illustrate rigidity, not a sporadic, extremal, or degenerate object
SL_n(Z) for n≥3 is a standard infinite-family higher-rank arithmetic lattice, introduced to illustrate rigidity of its representations.
It is used through its associated character variety, which is finite or essentially a single rigid point by higher-rank superrigidity.
For the higher-rank lattice the relevant character variety is rigid and finite, essentially a minimal-dimensional point case.
In the talk it is used via its associated character variety, which is finite or essentially one rigid point.
SL_n(Z) for n≥3 is used as a rigid higher-rank case whose relevant character variety is finite/isolated
Used to illustrate rigidity for higher-rank lattices, not to refute a claim, and SL_n(Z) for n≥3 is a standard infinite-family arithmetic lattice.
It is used to illustrate superrigidity, with the associated character variety finite or one-point, a collapsed zero-dimensional extreme.
SL_n(Z) for n≥3 is used as a standard higher-rank rigid lattice example, not as a sporadic, limiting, or optimal object.
Superrigidity makes the relevant higher-rank-lattice character variety rigid and essentially a single point.
superrigidity makes the relevant character variety collapse to a finite, essentially one-point rigid locus

examples.name: Surface-group character varieties
examples.note: Surface groups provide the model in which finite-field point counting plus the Frobenius-Mednykh formula proves irreducibility and dimension, and the special nature of this example motivates replacing explicit formulas by averaging for random relators.
examples.engagement_reason: For surface groups the speaker writes the presentation, uses the Frobenius counting formula, combines it with Lang-Weil and zeta estimates, and traces how this yields irreducibility and the expected dimension.

passes.reason: Surface-group character varieties form a standard infinite family whose commutator presentation is used for computation rather than refutation.
surface-group character varieties form a systematic infinite family with special known formulas, but are not structurally exceptional or extremal in the stated sense
Surface-group character varieties form a systematic family with known irreducibility and dimension, serving as a benchmark rather than an obstruction.
Surface-group character varieties form a systematic infinite family used as a known comparison case, not a sporadic or boundary object.
Surface-group character varieties form a systematic infinite family used as a standard known case, not a sporadic, collapsed, or boundary example.
Surface-group character varieties form a standard infinite family with known irreducibility and dimension, not a sporadic or adversarial case.

Used as a well-studied comparison case with known irreducibility and dimension formulas, not as an obstruction, and surface-group character varieties form a systematic family.
Surface-group character varieties form a systematic benchmark family with expected dimension and irreducibility, not an adversarial case.
Surface-group character varieties form a systematic benchmark family with a special commutator relator, but not a classification exception or extremal case.
Surface-group character varieties are a standard infinite family used as a tractable benchmark, not as a sporadic, boundary, or collapsed case.
surface-group character varieties form a systematic highly regular family governed by the special product-of-commutators relator

examples.name: Two-generator one-relator random group in SL2(C)
examples.note: The speaker specializes to k=2 and r=1 to illustrate the critical case where the random character variety is nonempty but zero-dimensional.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker specializes the random-group theorem to k=2, r=1 in SL2(C), explains that it falls in the middle case, and applies the theorem to conclude nonemptiness and existence of Zariski-dense two-generator representations satisfying the random relation.

passes.reason: The two-generator one-relator case has r=k−1, the critical boundary between empty and positive-dimensional random character varieties.
the two-generator one-relator random case is the critical threshold r=k-1 where the expected character variety becomes zero-dimensional but remains nonempty

With k=2 and r=1 it is the critical r=k-1 case, where the random SL2 character variety is nonempty but zero-dimensional/finite.
For k=2,r=1 it is the critical r=k−1 random-relator case, giving a finite nonempty SL2 character variety.

examples.name: Random-polynomial analogy for real or compact points
examples.note: In response to a question about whether representations land in SU(2) or real forms, the speaker compares the model to random polynomials and notes the SL2 trace polynomial of a random word as an analogy rather than a proof ingredient.
examples.engagement_reason: The random-polynomial model is used as an analogy for questions about real or compact points and expected proportions, but no specific polynomial model is computed or analyzed step by step.

passes.reason: The random-polynomial model is a typical heuristic analogy for real or unit-circle points, not a special obstruction.
